16: Jonathan's final act of covenant friendship is described with the verb ḥizzēq (to strengthen, make firm): wayyḥazzēq ʾet yāḏô bēlōhîm — 'he strengthened his hand in God.' Jonathan comes to David at his lowest point — hunted, in the wilderness, with Saul's army closing in — and his single act is not to bring supplies or military intelligence but to strengthen David's grip on God. This is the NT ministry of intercession at its highest form. Hebrews 7:25 describes Christ's ongoing high-priestly work in identical terms: 'he always lives to make intercession for them' — the resurrected Christ perpetually ḥizzēq-ing the hands of his people in God. Romans 8:34 places this intercession at the right hand of the Father. Jonathan's act — coming to the fugitive anointed at his hour of need to strengthen his hand in God — is the OT narrative type of Christ's unceasing intercession for those who belong to him in the hour of their trial and despair.
1: David hears that the Philistines are attacking Keilah and looting the threshing floors (šōsîm ʾet haggōrānôt). His instinct is to fight — to protect a Judean city even while he is a fugitive from Saul. The willingness of the anointed-but-not-yet-enthroned king to act in kingly defense of the people anticipates the pattern of servant-kingship: David exercises royal protection before possessing royal authority. Jesus heals and feeds people before the cross establishes his throne.
2: David inquires of YHWH through the ephod: hāʾēlēḵ wᵉhikkîtî bappᵉlištîm hāʾēlleh — shall I go and strike these Philistines? The answer is yes: lēḵ wᵉhikkîtā bappᵉlištîm wᵉhôšaʿtā ʾet Qᵉʿîlāh — go, strike the Philistines and save Keilah. The ephod-consultation sequence (go → inquiry → divine confirmation → action) establishes Spirit-guided mission. The Spirit's direction of the anointed's mission is the OT prototype for the NT's Spirit-led apostolic mission (Acts 13:2-4; 1 Samuel 16:6-10).
3: David's men resist: hinnēh ʾănaḥnû pōh bîhûdāh yᵉrēʾîm ûmah kî nēlēḵ Qᵉʿîlāh ʾel maʿarḵôt Pᵉlištîm — we are afraid here in Judah; how much more if we go to Keilah against the Philistine forces? The disciples' fear — they can barely survive where they are, let alone take on a new fight — is the natural human calculation against costly obedience. David inquires again (v4) and receives the same answer: qûm rēd Qᵉʿîlāh kî ʾānōḵî nōtēn ʾet Pᵉlištîm bᵉyādeḵā — rise, go down to Keilah, for I am giving the Philistines into your hand.
4: The double inquiry of YHWH (vv2-4) establishes the model of persistent prophetic consultation. The first confirmation overcomes theological doubt; the second overcomes human fear. The pattern — divine command, human hesitation, recommitment — occurs throughout the prophetic tradition (Jonah 3:1-3, repeating the commission after Jonah's flight) and in the disciples' commissioning (Acts 1:8 → Acts 8:1 → the actual scattering that produces the mission).
5: David and his men go to Keilah, fight the Philistines, and rescue the city: wayyôšaʿ dāwid ʾet yōšᵉḇê Qᵉʿîlāh — David saved the inhabitants of Keilah. The verb yāšaʿ (save) applied to David's military rescue recurs as the theological signature of the anointed: David is a yôšîaʿ, a deliverer. The messianic name Yeshua/Jesus embeds this deliverer-function in the person of the ultimate Anointed (hûʾ yôšîaʿ ʾet ʿammô mēḥaṭṭōʾtêhem, Matthew 1:21).
6: Abiathar the priest, who brought the ephod to David at Keilah, is the direct link between the Nob massacre's survivor and the ongoing prophetic guidance of the fugitive king. The ephod — the priestly instrument of divine inquiry — is now with the true king rather than in the destroyed priestly city. The transfer of the ephod from Nob to David's camp fulfills the prophetic sentence against the house of Eli (1 Samuel 2:27-36): the priestly line that served Saul is destroyed; the ephod that enables divine guidance migrates to the true king.
7: Saul hears David is in Keilah and concludes that God has delivered him into his hand: nāḵar ʾōtô ʾĕlōhîm bᵉyādî kî nisgār lāḇōʾ bᵉʿîr daltayim ûḇᵉrîaḥ — God has given him into my hand, for he has shut himself in by entering a city with gates and bars. Saul's misreading of providence — interpreting David's presence in a walled city as divine gift rather than divine trap for Saul himself — demonstrates the persecutor's theological confusion. He sees confirmation of his purpose where there is actually the working out of his judgment.
8: Saul summons all the people to go down to Keilah and besiege David: the full military apparatus of the king marshaled against a single fugitive saving a Judean city. The disproportion is the narrative's theological point: the illegitimate king's full power pursues the legitimate king who just rescued the city the pursuer should have protected.
9: David, knowing Saul's evil design (kî ʿālāyw Šāʾûl mᵉḥārēš hārāʿāh), says to Abiathar: bring the ephod. The knowledge of the evil plan, obtained through some prophetic channel before the full inquiry, drives the consultation. The sequence — knowledge of threat → prophetic inquiry → divine answer → escape — is the pattern of Jesus's post-resurrection appearances: he knows what the disciples need before they ask (John 21:6).
10: David's prayer: YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl šāmōaʿ šāmaʿ ʿaḇdᵉḵā kî šāmōaʿ šāmaʿ Šāʾûl mᵉḇaqqēš lāḇôʾ ʾel Qᵉʿîlāh lᵉšaḥēt lāʿîr bᵉʿăḇūrî — YHWH God of Israel, your servant has heard that Saul seeks to come to Keilah to destroy the city on my account. David's prayer places his own welfare last — his primary concern is the destruction of Keilah on his account. The anointed takes on responsibility for the city's jeopardy rather than merely seeking his own escape. This other-centered framing of the prayer defines the messianic vocation.
11: The double question to YHWH: will the men of Keilah surrender David to Saul? Will Saul come down? YHWH answers both: yāred — he will come down; yasgîrû — they will surrender you. The answer is a prophetic disclosure of future human decisions — YHWH knows what Keilah will choose before they choose it. The divine foreknowledge of human betrayal (not coercion, but foreknowledge) parallels Jesus's prediction of Judas's betrayal (John 13:21) and Peter's denial (Matthew 26:34) — the omniscient Anointed knows who will betray him and yet proceeds.
12: David and his men depart: the city of Keilah is rescued but will not protect its rescuer. The beneficiaries of the anointed's salvation repay with betrayal. This pattern — saving those who will subsequently abandon you — is fundamental to the Davidic-messianic vocation. Jesus saves and is abandoned: they all fled (Mark 14:50). The anointed's salvation does not depend on the gratitude of the saved.
13: David and about 600 men depart from Keilah. The number 600 is significant: it matches the number of Saul's initial fighting force (1 Samuel 13:15) and the Danite warriors (Judges 18:11). The fugitive army is a mirror-kingdom — the true king with a fighting force equal to the recognized king's initial muster. When Saul hears David has escaped Keilah, the campaign is abandoned. The wayyitnāḥēm (and he was sorry, abandoned the plan) is the same verb used for YHWH's relenting — here applied to Saul's frustrated purpose.
14: David stays in wilderness strongholds (mᵉṣûdôt) in the hill country of Ziph. He is in the wilderness day by day, and Saul seeks him every day, but YHWH does not give him into his hand (wᵉlōʾ nᵉtānô ʾĕlōhîm bᵉyādô). The summary verse encapsulates the entire fugitive period's theology: Saul's relentless pursuit, David's daily danger, and YHWH's daily deliverance. The anointed is preserved through the wilderness by divine protection that operates invisibly — just as Jesus's many escapes from arrest in John's Gospel (1 Samuel 7:30; 1 Samuel 8:59; 1 Samuel 10:39) reflect the same providential protection: his hour had not yet come.
15: David sees (wayyarᵉ dāwid) that Saul has come to seek his life at Horesh in the wilderness of Ziph. The fugitive's awareness of the approaching threat precedes any action. The sight of the pursuing king — at the moment of greatest danger — is immediately followed by Jonathan's arrival (v16). The juxtaposition is the narrative's signature of divine provision: the moment of maximum threat coincides with the arrival of maximum encouragement.
17: Jonathan's covenant renewal with David at Horesh: ʾal-tîrāʾ kî lōʾ timṣāʾʾăḵā yad Šāʾûl ʾāḇî wᵉʾattāh timlōḵ ʿal yiśrāʾēl wᵉʾānōḵî ʾehyeh-lᵉḵā lᵉmišneh — do not fear; for the hand of Saul my father shall not find you. You shall be king over Israel, and I shall be second to you. Jonathan's explicit acknowledgment — you shall be king, I shall be second — is the covenant surrender of his own succession rights. He yields what is his by birth to the anointed. John the Baptist makes this same surrender explicit: He must increase, but I must decrease (John 3:30). The one who holds the birthright by human order yields to the one anointed by divine choice.
18: wayyikrᵉtû šᵉnêhem bᵉrît lipnê YHWH — and the two of them made a covenant before YHWH. This is the second or third covenant between David and Jonathan (1 Samuel 18:3; 1 Samuel 20:8; now 1 Samuel 23:18). The covenant's renewal at the moment of David's greatest vulnerability reaffirms what was made at the court's height. Covenant made in prosperity and then reaffirmed in danger is the pattern of divine faithfulness: YHWH's covenant with Israel is renewed in the Sinai desert (Exod 19-24), in Moab before conquest (Deut), and in exile (Jeremiah 31:31-34) — the new covenant made precisely when the old seems broken.
19: The Ziphites betray David to Saul: wayyadᵉbîrû ʾel Šāʾûl baGiḇʿāh lēʾmōr hălōʾ Dāwid mistattēr ʿimānû bᵉmᵉṣûdôt baḥōrᵉšāh — is not David hiding with us in the strongholds at Horesh? The Ziphites are Judeans — their own tribesmen betray the fugitive king. The betrayal from within the tribal community mirrors the betrayals in Jesus's ministry: his family thinks he is insane (Mark 3:21), his hometown rejects him (Luke 4:28-29), and a member of his inner circle betrays him (Matthew 26:14-16).
20: The Ziphites offer to hand David over to Saul: wᵉʿattāh kᵉḵol ʾāwen napšᵉḵā hammélek rᵉdat rēd ûlānû hammaśgîr bᵉyad hammélek — now come down, O king, according to all your heart's desire to come down, and our part shall be to surrender him to the king's hand. The volunteered informing goes beyond required loyalty to active complicity. The Ziphites' offer mirrors those who volunteered to identify Jesus to the authorities (John 11:57) — official policy being implemented by the willing among the populace.
21: Saul's response: bᵉrûḵîm ʾattem laYHWH kî ḥamaltem ʿālay — blessed are you by YHWH because you have had compassion on me. The inverted blessing — invoking YHWH's name in gratitude for betrayal of the anointed — is a form of theological corruption. Saul uses YHWH-language for actions YHWH opposes. The persecution of God's anointed done in God's name is a recurring pattern: the Pharisees claim to honor Moses while rejecting the one Moses anticipated (John 5:45-46).
22: Saul instructs the Ziphites to confirm David's hiding places carefully: wᵉdᵉʿû ûrᵉʾû ʾet mᵉqômô ʾăšer tihyeh raglô — know and see the place where his foot is. The surveillance vocabulary — tracking the physical location of the fugitive — echoes throughout the passion narratives: the chief priests seeking the right time and place to seize Jesus (Luke 22:2), Judas providing the location of the garden (John 18:2).
23: Saul mobilizes a comprehensive search: ûrᵉʾû wᵉhišmaʿtem ʾet kol hamᵉḥabbōʾîm — take note of all the lurking places where he hides. The intelligence-gathering apparatus of the state directed against a single fugitive demonstrates how threatening the true anointed is to the false king. The more thorough the surveillance, the more complete the eventual failure — divine preservation outpaces human intelligence.
24: The Ziphites set out ahead of Saul. David and his men are in the wilderness of Maon (bᵉmiḏbar Māʿôn bāʿărāḇāh ʾel yᵉmin hayyᵉšîmôn). The geography shifts: from Horesh to Maon, the fugitive moves further into the desert. The desert wilderness as the location of the anointed's hiding and testing is the OT template for Jesus's forty-day wilderness testing (Matthew 4:1) — the anointed proved in the wasteland before entering his public ministry.
25: Saul and his men go to search, and David hears: wayyēreḏ hassela wayyēšeḇ bᵉmiḏbar Māʿôn — David went down to the rock and dwelt in the wilderness of Maon. The topographic detail — David going down to the rock — carries typological resonance: the rock (sela) as divine refuge is the Psalms' signature metaphor for YHWH (Psalms 18:2, 31, 46; 1 Samuel 19:14; 1 Samuel 62:2, 7). David literally lives in the rock that typologically expresses YHWH as refuge.
26: Saul goes on one side of the mountain while David and his men are on the other: wayhî Šāʾûl hōlēḵ mizzeh hahār ûdāwid wᵉʾanāšāyw mizzeh hahār. David is hurrying to escape, Saul is encircling him. wayhî dāwid maḥîš lāleḵet mippᵉnê Šāʾûl — David was hurrying. The noun maḥîš (hurrying) captures the urgency of the chase at its climax. The anointed's preservation through geography — the same mountain, opposite sides — is pure providential timing.
27: A messenger comes to Saul: māhēr ûlēḵ kî pāšᵉṭû Pᵉlištîm ʿal hāʾāreṣ — hurry and come, for the Philistines have raided the land. The Philistine raid that arrives at the exact moment of David's encirclement is the providential rescue — an external threat redirects the pursuer at the last possible moment. YHWH does not need to appear; he uses the ordinary mechanism of geopolitical emergency to preserve his anointed. The same pattern operates in the passion narrative's timing: events beyond anyone's control converge to accomplish the purposes of YHWH.
28: Saul withdraws from pursuing David to engage the Philistines: wayyāšob Šāʾûl mirdōp ʾaḥărê dāwid wayyēlek liqraʾt Pᵉlištîm. The place is named Sela-hammahlekot — the Rock of Division — because the king and David were divided there. The naming preserves the theological memory: YHWH divided the mountain between the pursuer and the pursued. The name is both geographical record and theological testimony.
29: David goes up from there and lives in the strongholds of En-gedi (bᵉmᵉṣûdôt ʿEn Gedî). The En-gedi wilderness — on the western shore of the Dead Sea — is among the most rugged terrain in the land, with springs and caves amidst sheer cliffs. The withdrawal to En-gedi is withdrawal to the strongest natural defensive position. The next scene (1 Samuel 24) will place the pursuer and the pursued in the same cave at En-gedi — the reversal of the mountain-separation of v28 will become the cave-proximity that tests the anointed's restraint.
